name: feat-ESE-0012-06
title: Multi-pass GPU physics: broadphase + narrowphase + integration
description:
Implement multi-pass GPU physics with the physics authority model: broadphase collision detection via spatial hashing, narrowphase sphere-sphere intersection, and Verlet integration with configurable substeps. All passes execute in a single command encoder submission with no CPU round-trips.

Physics has sole write authority over Transform and Velocity for entities with GpuRigidBody. Game logic communicates via intent components: GpuForce (accumulated forces), GpuImpulse (instant velocity change), GpuTeleport (position override). The integration kernel reads intents, applies them, integrates, and zeroes consumed intents.

Broadphase: uniform grid spatial hash with atomic insertion (Unity GPU Physics pattern). Narrowphase: 27-neighbor cell search with sphere-sphere tests. Integration: Verlet with N substeps in a GPU loop, consuming intents on first substep.

References: GPU Gems Ch.32, Unity GPU Physics (7-kernel pipeline), Godot's RigidBody authority model. See engine/gpu/architecture.md sections 7 and 8.
acceptanceCriteria:
- GpuRigidBody and GpuCollider (sphere) components defined (gpu-prefixed)
- GpuForce, GpuImpulse, GpuTeleport intent components defined
- Broadphase kernel: spatial hash grid with atomic compare-exchange insertion, 4 entities per cell
- Narrowphase kernel: 27-neighbor search, sphere-sphere overlap, impulse response writes to Velocity
- Integration kernel: reads GpuForce/GpuImpulse/GpuTeleport, applies Verlet integration with configurable substep count, zeroes consumed intents
- All passes dispatch in single command encoder (clear, populate, detect, integrate)
- GPU physics writes to shared Transform + Velocity; CPU rendering reads after readback
- CPU game logic applies forces/impulses via intent components, NOT by writing Transform directly
- Authority guard: CPU write to Transform on GpuRigidBody entity throws in dev mode
- 500+ rigid bodies colliding at stable framerate
- Collision pairs match brute-force CPU reference
demoDeliverable: Browser demo: 500+ bouncing spheres in a box. Spheres spawn from top, fall under gravity, collide with each other and walls, settle into piles. Toggle substep count (1, 4, 8) via UI to show stability improvement. Interactive: click to apply GpuImpulse to a sphere (CPU writes intent, GPU applies it next frame). Button to teleport a sphere to a random position via GpuTeleport. Console logs collision pair count per frame and GPU dispatch time. Debug overlay shows spatial hash grid occupancy.
testingScenarios:
- Broadphase: 2 overlapping spheres in same grid cell detected as collision pair
- Broadphase: 2 spheres in distant cells produce no collision pair
- Narrowphase: sphere-sphere impulse response conserves momentum within epsilon
- Integration: single sphere under gravity follows expected parabolic trajectory
- Substeps: 8 substeps produces more stable stacking than 1 substep
- Intent: CPU applies GpuForce(10,0,0) — sphere accelerates in X, force zeroed after consumption
- Intent: CPU applies GpuImpulse(0,20,0) — sphere jumps, impulse zeroed after consumption
- Intent: CPU sets GpuTeleport(50,50,50,active=1) — sphere moves to (50,50,50), active cleared
- Authority guard: CPU write to Transform on GpuRigidBody entity throws
- Grid capacity: >4 entities in one cell handled gracefully
- Readback: CPU-side Transform matches GPU-computed positions after physics pass
stakeholderUnderstanding: Multi-pass physics proves the system handles real workloads — multiple compute passes in a single GPU submission with no CPU round-trips between them. The broadphase spatial hash + narrowphase collision + Verlet integration pipeline is the pattern for all future GPU physics. Configurable substeps show how GPU loops avoid the CPU bottleneck of per-substep dispatch. This is the payoff ticket — if this works, the engine has production-grade GPU physics.
